fragments.content: The FDA has multiple approval pathways with different review timelines and evidence requirements. Standard review applies to drugs offering modest improvements over existing treatments, with a 10-month review clock from NDA submission. Priority review (6-month clock) is granted for drugs treating serious conditions with potential significant improvements in safety or efficacy. Breakthrough Therapy designation (for serious/life-threatening conditions) provides intensive FDA guidance, organizational commitment, and eligibility for accelerated approval if preliminary clinical evidence shows substantial improvement over available therapies.

The FDA review process begins with pre-NDA meetings to align on data requirements. New Drug Application (NDA) submission includes all preclinical and clinical data, proposed labeling, manufacturing information, and risk management plans. FDA conducts filing review (60 days) to determine if the application is complete. If filed, substantive review evaluates efficacy (adequate and well-controlled trials), safety (adverse event profile, risk-benefit), and CMC (chemistry, manufacturing, controls). Advisory Committee meetings may be convened for external expert input. Facility inspections verify GMP compliance. Review outcomes include approval, complete response letter (CRL) detailing deficiencies, or withdrawal by sponsor.

FDA approval criteria require: substantial evidence of effectiveness from adequate and well-controlled investigations (typically two Phase III trials or one exceptionally robust trial), acceptable safety profile where benefits outweigh risks, appropriate labeling reflecting indications, dosing, warnings, and adverse reactions, and compliant manufacturing under cGMP. Priority review criteria include: ability to treat serious conditions, potential for significant improvement over existing therapies (e.g., enhanced efficacy, reduced toxicity, improved adherence). Breakthrough Therapy criteria require: treatment of serious/life-threatening condition, preliminary clinical evidence indicating substantial improvement on clinically significant endpoints. Approximately 90% of standard review and 95% of priority review applications are approved within their respective timelines.
